summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the Windows security epidemic stems from Microsoft's decision to make Administrator the default user account, dating back to Windows NT 3.0. He demonstrates how a completely unpatched Windows XP machine can be infected through drive-by browser exploits simply by visiting a webpage, but proves this attack is completely ineffective when logged in as a standard user. Atwood criticizes Microsoft for failing to make standard user accounts the default in Windows Vista, instead opting for the compromise of UAC prompts. He contends that setting up non-technical users as standard users is the single most important security measure for Windows machines.
key_points: Drive-by malware infections can occur simply by visiting a webpage with an unpatched browser, without clicking anything or downloading files
Running as a standard (non-administrator) user completely prevented the same infection that devastated an administrator account
Microsoft's original sin was making Administrator the default account type in Windows NT 3.0, a decision they perpetuated through Vista
The Windows software ecosystem has become so compromised that users cannot distinguish legitimate security software from the malware it claims to remove
Backwards compatibility concerns have led Microsoft to prioritize convenience over security, even when the security costs are enormous
money_quotes: When the only viable solution to sickness is to kill the patient, you have a problem of epidemic proportions.
It's nearly impossible to tell the good guys from the bad guys.
Choosing not to run as an Administrator is easily the single most important security tip for a Windows machine.
I think every technical computer user should have this experience, so they can see first hand, on their own machine, the profound evil that we're up against.
They'd probably have to buy a new computer.
I guess that's yet another thing we can sacrifice at the dark altar of backwards compatibility.
Microsoft didn't have the guts to make standard users the default – as they absolutely should have – in Windows Vista.
key_insight: The Windows security crisis is largely self-inflicted by Microsoft's decades-long refusal to default users to non-administrator accounts, a simple change that would prevent most drive-by malware infections.
